summary: Denmark's posture is led by the Centre for Cyber Security, whose theme page and news articles explain the transition to quantum-safe cryptography and the migration of Danish organisations to quantum-resistant algorithms. In 2024 the Centre established a dedicated secretariat, set up under the national quantum technology strategy, to help authorities and companies in Denmark's critical digital infrastructure make the transition. Parliament supports a coordinated European effort, noting quantum-safe cryptography alongside 5G and 6G in EU Council conclusions, and the central bank advises that preparations for the financial sector should be strengthened. No national migration timeline is attested in the gathered documents; the dated milestones reflect the EU coordinated roadmap rather than a Danish national one.
govActors:
CFCS | Centre for Cyber Security, sets out the transition to quantum-safe cryptography for Danish organisations and runs a dedicated secretariat established in 2024 to help critical digital infrastructure migrate
SAMSIK | Danish Agency for Societal Security, hosts the transition guidance on its newer domain
Ministry of Industry, Business and Financial Affairs | issued the National Strategy for Quantum Technology under which the CFCS quantum-safe cryptography secretariat sits
Folketinget | Danish Parliament, background and subsidiarity note supporting a coordinated EU effort and Council samlenotat noting quantum-safe cryptography alongside 5G and 6G
Nationalbanken | central bank, advises strengthening preparations for quantum-safe cryptography in the financial sector
processParticipation: NIS Cooperation Group | co-authored the EU coordinated PQC roadmap
migrationTimeline:
2030 | High-risk use cases migrated
2035 | Full migration of all systems complete
mainRegulation:
NIS2 Directive (EU) 2022/2555, Art. 21(2)(h) | EU | binding-law
DORA, Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 (financial sector) | EU | binding-law
Commission Recommendation (EU) 2024/1101 on a coordinated PQC roadmap | EU | soft-law
NIS Cooperation Group Coordinated Implementation Roadmap (2026/2030/2035) | EU | soft-law
obligation: Essential and important entities must use state-of-the-art cryptography under NIS2, transposed into national law, and financial entities face equivalent duties under DORA; neither yet names post-quantum algorithms specifically. Migration follows the EU coordinated roadmap, with high-risk use cases targeted for 2030 and full migration by 2035, which is encouraged rather than mandated.
